name: BGP Hold Timer
on_expiry: Peer declared dead; session torn down. All routes learned from the peer are withdrawn and re-evaluated by best-path selection.
tuning_advice: Use BFD for sub-second failure detection. Hold timer is a BGP-level fallback for environments where BFD is unavailable. Values below 15s are not recommended on high-CPU platforms due to keepalive jitter risk. Negotiated to the lower of the two peers' values.

name: BGP Keepalive Timer
on_expiry: A KEEPALIVE message is sent to the peer to reset the hold timer. If sending fails, the session is considered failed.
tuning_advice: Keepalive is always negotiated as hold/3. If you change the hold timer, the keepalive follows proportionally. Mismatched keepalive timers between peers are not an error; BGP uses the negotiated hold timer from OPEN message exchange.

name: BGP Connect-Retry Timer
on_expiry: A new TCP connection attempt is made to the configured peer. Used in the IDLE and CONNECT states to pace reconnection attempts after a session failure.
tuning_advice: Lower values (10–30s) speed up recovery after a peer restart but increase CPU load from connection churn on unstable links. FRR default in SONiC is 10s for most deployments; check running config. Governed by RFC 4271 ConnectRetryTimer.

name: OSPF Hello Interval
on_expiry: A Hello packet is sent out the interface. Used for neighbor discovery, keepalive, and DR/BDR election on multi-access networks.
tuning_advice: Hello interval must match between neighbors or adjacency will not form. Lowering to 1s with a corresponding dead interval of 4s speeds convergence but increases CPU on large broadcast domains. BFD is preferred for sub-second detection.

name: OSPF Dead Interval
on_expiry: Neighbor declared dead; adjacency torn down. All LSAs learned from the neighbor are aged out and SPF is triggered.
tuning_advice: Dead interval must be at least 4x the hello interval per RFC 2328 (enforced by FRR). Must match between neighbors. Use BFD for faster failure detection rather than tuning dead interval below 4s.

name: OSPF LSA MaxAge
on_expiry: LSA is flushed from the LSDB. If the originating router is still present, it re-originates the LSA with age 0. Used to bound LSDB size and prevent stale topology information.
tuning_advice: Not configurable in standard OSPF implementations including FRR. LSA refresh occurs at LSRefreshTime (default 1800s) before MaxAge is reached. Do not confuse LSA age with the flooding mechanism.

name: OSPF SPF Delay / Throttle
default: Initial delay: 0ms; Min hold: 50ms; Max hold: 5000ms (FRR defaults)
range: 0–600000ms for each of the three throttle parameters
on_expiry: SPF calculation is triggered after the delay expires post topology change. The hold time doubles after each successive trigger (exponential backoff) up to the max-hold value.
tuning_advice: FRR uses a three-parameter SPF throttle (delay, min-hold, max-hold). Tune with 'timers throttle spf'. Low initial delay (0–50ms) is good for fast convergence; ensure max-hold caps churn under instability.

name: BFD Detection Multiplier
on_expiry: N/A — this is a multiplier, not a countdown. Detection time = remote tx-interval * local detect-multiplier. When no control packets are received for the detection time, the session declares DOWN.
tuning_advice: Lower multiplier (2) gives faster detection but is less tolerant of packet loss. Multiplier of 3 is standard; 5 is used on lossy links or high-CPU platforms. Asymmetric multipliers between peers are allowed.

name: BFD Desired Min Tx Interval
range: 50–60000ms (platform-dependent; hardware BFD may support lower values)
on_expiry: A BFD control packet is sent to the peer. The actual transmitted interval is max(local desired-min-tx, remote required-min-rx).
tuning_advice: 300ms is the recommended starting point for software BFD. For hardware-offloaded BFD on supported ASICs (Broadcom DNX, Mellanox Spectrum), 50–100ms is achievable. Sub-50ms requires hardware BFD support. Coordinate with the peer's required-min-rx.

name: BFD Required Min Rx Interval
on_expiry: N/A — this is the minimum rate at which the local system is willing to receive BFD packets. Actual rx interval is negotiated as max(local required-min-rx, remote desired-min-tx).
tuning_advice: Set equal to tx-interval for symmetric sessions. Increasing required-min-rx tells the peer it may send less frequently, reducing CPU load on congested control planes.

name: LACP Actor Timeout (Short/Long)
range: Long or Short (not an arbitrary number; defined by IEEE 802.3ad)
on_expiry: Partner declared expired; LAG member port is removed from the active aggregation. In SHORT timeout mode the actor expects LACPDUs every 1s (FAST rate); in LONG mode every 30s (SLOW rate).
tuning_advice: Use SHORT (fast) timeout for LAG links to upstream spines where failure detection speed matters. LONG timeout reduces control-plane overhead on stable links. Both ends must agree on the timeout mode or one side will rapidly expire the other.
sonic_config_path: CONFIG_DB:PORTCHANNEL|<portchannel>:lacp_key (fast_rate not separately stored; set via teammgrd)
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — LACP is handled by teamd, not FRR

name: LACP PDU Transmission Interval
range: 1s (fast) or 30s (slow) — IEEE 802.3ad defines only these two rates
on_expiry: A LACPDU (Link Aggregation Control Protocol Data Unit) is transmitted to the partner. Contains the actor's system priority, key, port priority, and state flags.
tuning_advice: Fast rate is recommended for spine-leaf fabrics. Slow rate is acceptable for static or low-churn environments. The PDU interval is determined by the partner's timeout mode preference sent in the LACPDU.
sonic_default: Slow rate (30s) unless fast_rate is configured

name: LLDP Transmit Interval
on_expiry: An LLDPDU (LLDP Data Unit) is transmitted out each enabled interface. Carries chassis ID, port ID, TTL, system name, capabilities, and management address TLVs.
tuning_advice: 30s is the standard value per IEEE 802.1AB. Lower values (5–10s) are used in environments requiring faster topology discovery updates (e.g., automated provisioning systems). TTL is tx-interval * tx-hold.
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — managed by lldpd (lldpcli configure lldp tx-interval <seconds>)

name: LLDP Transmit Hold Multiplier
on_expiry: N/A — multiplier used to compute TTL: TTL = tx-interval * tx-hold (default: 30 * 4 = 120s). When a neighbor's LLDPDU TTL expires without refresh, the neighbor entry is removed from the LLDP MIB.
tuning_advice: Increasing tx-hold increases the TTL sent in LLDPDUs, giving neighbors more time before purging stale entries. Useful on flaky links. Do not set below 2.
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — lldpcli configure lldp tx-hold <value>

name: LLDP Re-initialization Delay
on_expiry: After an LLDP port is disabled and then re-enabled, this delay must elapse before LLDP transmission resumes. Prevents LLDP frame storms during rapid link flapping.
tuning_advice: Leave at default (2s) in most deployments. Increase only if observing LLDP churn during LAG reconvergence or STP topology changes.
sonic_config_path: Not exposed in CONFIG_DB; lldpd default applies
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — lldpcli configure lldp reinit <seconds>

name: STP/RSTP Hello Timer
on_expiry: The root bridge sends a BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) out all designated ports. Non-root bridges relay BPDUs downstream. Used to confirm root bridge liveness and maintain the spanning tree topology.
tuning_advice: Do not change the hello timer without adjusting forward-delay and max-age proportionally to satisfy the 802.1D stability criteria: 2*(forward-delay - 1) >= max-age, 2*(hello + 1) <= max-age. Mismatched timers cause topology instability. RSTP largely replaces timer-based convergence with rapid state machines; timer tuning matters more in legacy STP environments.
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — STP is handled by the SONiC STP container (StpMgrd), not FRR

name: STP/RSTP Forward Delay
on_expiry: A port transitions from Listening to Learning (after first expiry) and from Learning to Forwarding (after second expiry) in classic STP. In RSTP, the forward-delay is used only as a fallback when rapid transition is not available (e.g., on edge ports or with legacy 802.1D bridges).
tuning_advice: In pure RSTP environments, most ports converge in <1s via the proposal/agreement handshake and forward-delay is rarely the bottleneck. Tuning matters in mixed STP/RSTP environments or when RSTP rapid transition fails due to misconfiguration.
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — managed by SONiC STP container

name: STP/RSTP Max Age
on_expiry: A non-root bridge declares that BPDUs from the root are stale. The bridge assumes the root has failed and initiates a topology change by re-running root bridge election. In RSTP, max-age bounds the time before a port without recent BPDUs becomes a Designated Port.
tuning_advice: The relationship 2*(forward-delay - 1) >= max-age must hold. Reducing max-age below 20s risks spurious topology changes on congested control planes where BPDU delivery is delayed. In RSTP, rely on hello-interval and proposal/agreement rather than tuning max-age.
sonic_frr_path: Not applicable — managed by SONiC STP container

name: MC-LAG Keepalive Interval
on_expiry: A keepalive message is sent to the MC-LAG peer over the keepalive link (separate from the peer link). If the peer does not respond within the session-timeout, it is declared dead and failover begins.
tuning_advice: 1s is the recommended default. Lower values (sub-second) are not supported by SONiC iccpd. Higher values increase failover time. The keepalive link should be a dedicated L3 connection (not over the peer link) for independent failure detection.

name: MC-LAG Session Timeout
on_expiry: MC-LAG peer declared dead. The surviving peer assumes sole ownership of all MC-LAG port-channels, withdraws the peer's MAC addresses, and begins forwarding traffic as a standalone device. Orphan ports on the failed peer lose connectivity.
tuning_advice: Session timeout must be at least 3x the keepalive interval. 15s provides a balance between false-positive avoidance and fast failover. In stable environments with dedicated keepalive links, 10s is reasonable. Never set below 3s.
